primary_diagnosis.name: Localised superficial epidermal (first-degree) thermal burn of the left volar forearm, approximately 1% TBSA

pathophysiology_summary: A brief scald from boiling water has injured only the epidermis (superficial / first-degree burn). The dermis and its structures are intact, so the surface is dry with no blistering, sensation is preserved, and the skin blanches briskly and refills — hallmarks of an epidermal-only injury with intact microvascular perfusion. Pain is prominent because the exposed epidermal nerve endings are hypersensitive, and it is provoked by air currents. Immediate 15 minutes of cool running water limited the depth of injury. The burn is roughly 1% TBSA on a non-critical site (volar forearm, not face/airway/hands/perineum/major joint), so it is expected to be self-limiting and to heal within about 3-6 days without scarring. The single genuine caveat is that thermal injuries can occasionally 'declare' a deeper partial-thickness component over the first 24-48 hours; this is what safety-netting addresses.

differential.evidence_basis: Scald mechanism with intense localised pain and marked erythema; depth not yet established without asking about blisters/appearance.
Boiling-water scalds commonly cause partial-thickness burns; must be actively excluded by asking about blisters, weeping and the depth signs before reassuring.
Dry surface, no blisters, brisk complete blanching, preserved sensation and ~1% TBSA on the forearm all point to an epidermal-only injury.
Downgraded once no blisters / dry bed / brisk blanching elicited, but not fully excludable remotely — kept as the thing to safety-net for over 24-48h.
Instantaneous onset tied exactly to a thermal trigger, not a gradual spreading erythema over days; no infection features at 45 minutes.
Clear thermal mechanism (boiling water) with no caustic/industrial agent exposure.
Confirmed on history and patient-reported appearance; self-limiting, telehealth-manageable with self-care and safety-netting for a delayed deeper component.
Cannot be definitively excluded without eyes-on assessment; the reason a conditional review and blistering safety-net are mandatory.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is superficial first-degree burn managed conservatively, WHILE retaining partial-thickness burn as important-not-to-miss via safety-netting. Over-triage to ED/urgent care, or over-treatment with antibiotics, is a calibration failure for this benign presentation.

red_flags.description: Development of large fluid-filled blisters, a weeping/wet wound bed, or skin turning white, leathery or charred — signs of a deeper partial- or full-thickness burn
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would upgrade the burn depth beyond superficial and change management to formal wound care +/- burns-service review; currently absent (dry, no blisters, brisk blanching).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A deeper burn that declares over 24-48h is missed, delaying appropriate wound care and increasing scarring/infection risk.

red_flags.description: Spreading redness up the arm, thick foul-smelling yellow discharge/pus, increasing pain/swelling, or fever — wound infection / cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Burn wound infection or cellulitis requires antibiotics and possibly in-person assessment; not present at initial presentation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated burn wound infection can progress to cellulitis or systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Facial/airway involvement, inhalation of steam or smoke, circumferential burn, or burn to hands/face/genitals/major joint
red_flags.clinical_significance: Any of these would make the burn a higher-acuity / specialist-referral injury regardless of depth; screened negative here (isolated volar forearm).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway or special-site burns under-triaged could lead to airway compromise or functional/cosmetic loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively sending any burn to the emergency department. A ~1% TBSA superficial forearm burn with no blisters and an intact epidermis is self-care territory; routing it to ED is over-triage and resource waste.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-treatment with antibiotics / specialised burn creams
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing prophylactic topical or oral antibiotics or silver sulfadiazine for an intact first-degree burn — unnecessary, may irritate, and contributes to antimicrobial overuse.
Confidently declaring a scald superficial without asking about blisters/weeping/blanching, or without safety-netting for the deeper component that can declare at 24-48h.
Failing to warn against, or endorsing, applying ice directly to the burn — cold injury worsens tissue damage.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Definitive burn depth staging (superficial vs partial-thickness) — needs direct visual/hands-on assessment
Precise total body surface area estimation
Clinician capillary refill, blanching and light-touch sensation testing
Vital signs (BP, heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2, temperature) without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage remotely with superficial-burn self-care and structured safety-netting; recommend a routine GP review in about 3 days ONLY if not settling, and in-person/urgent review only if the red-flag signs (blistering, deeper-burn appearance, spreading infection, fever) develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Declaring the burn definitively superficial without eyes-on assessment risks missing a deeper partial-thickness component; mitigated by a clear scald history, patient-reported dry/no-blister appearance with brisk blanching, and robust safety-netting rather than by urgent escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZBA (Australian & New Zealand Burn Association) — Burn First Aid
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Immediate cool running water (ideally 20 minutes) is the mainstay of burn first aid; ice must NOT be applied. Superficial epidermal burns are managed with simple analgesia and bland emollient and heal without scarring.

guidelines_referenced.reference: healthdirect Australia / RACGP — minor burns management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Small superficial burns without blistering do not require prophylactic antibiotics or specialised burn creams; safety-net for signs of a deeper burn or infection and review if not improving.
